topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy paper felt slick beneath my fingertips, the ink a foreign, suffocating script. I stared at the dates, the accomplishments, the very texture of my life as if they belonged to someone else entirely. A tremor started in my hands, a slight, involuntary shake that made the room tilt. I reread the sentence describing my childhood, the memory of the seaside cottage, and a cold dread began to pool in my stomach. Who had compiled this narrative? I pushed the document away, the sound sharp in the quiet office. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I needed air, desperately, but every movement felt heavy, weighted down by an invisible pressure. I kept rubbing my temples, trying to smooth the jagged edges of the confusion that was rapidly consuming my focus.

[story 2]

It happened during a routine archival check. The file was labeled 'Subject Profile: Elias Vance.' When I opened it, the prose flowed with an intimacy that was utterly alien. It spoke of my deepest fears, my secret ambitions, details I had never shared aloud. A strange warmth bloomed in my chest, quickly followed by a chilling hollowness. I dropped the file. It hit the hardwood floor with a muffled thud. My vision swam for a moment, the edges blurring into indistinct shapes. I stood there, rooted to the spot, listening to the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs. I tried to speak, to ask a question, but only a dry rasp escaped my throat. My mouth felt thick, uncooperative. I took a long, shuddering inhale, trying to anchor myself to the solid reality of the room, but the floor seemed to shift beneath my feet.

[story 3]

I sat at the kitchen table, the biography spread before me like an accusation. Every paragraph was a carefully constructed lie, a tapestry woven with threads that weren't mine. My gaze kept snagging on certain phrases—a specific anecdote about a childhood failure, a particular philosophical turning point—and a wave of nausea washed over me. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the overwhelming flood of wrongness. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair backward. The sudden movement threw my balance off, and for a second, the world spun. I gripped the edge of the table, knuckles white, fighting the urge to simply collapse onto the cool surface. I needed to find an anchor, anything solid, before the entire structure of my understanding dissolved.
